name: Binary Judgments for Evals
one_liner: Design LLM‑based evaluators to output binary pass/fail decisions instead of rating scales for clearer, more actionable metrics.
definition: The practice of constraining LLM‑as‑a‑Judge prompts to produce a simple true/false or pass/fail verdict on a specific failure mode, rather than a numeric score or rating.
explanation: Hamel argues that binary decisions force clarity: 'you want to make it binary because we want to simplify things. We don't want, ‘Hey, score this on a rating of one to five. How good is it?’ That's just in most cases, that's a weasel way of not making a decision.' Shreya reinforces that a tight scope makes LLM judges 'very capable of doing very reliably.'
when_to_use: When you need clear, actionable signal on whether a failure occurred
When you want to avoid the ambiguity of interpreting average scores
When the failure mode is well‑defined enough to make a yes/no judgment
when_not_to_use: When the quality dimension truly exists on a continuous spectrum and a binary threshold would lose important information (but the speakers argue most cases benefit from binary)
When you need graded feedback for model improvement beyond just pass/fail
metric_or_method: Prompt the LLM evaluator to return a boolean or ‘true’/‘false’ based on explicit criteria, avoiding Likert scales or numerical ratings.
common_pitfalls: Falling back to rating scales because it's easier than defining a precise binary condition
Reporting ambiguous metrics like ‘3.2 average score’, which are hard to interpret and act upon
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
source_excerpt: you want to make it binary because we want to simplify things. We don't want, 'Hey, score this on a rating of one to five. How good is it?' That's just in most cases, that's a weasel way of not making a decision. Like, 'No, you need to make a decision. Is this good enough or not? Yes or no?' [...] when you report these metrics, no one knows what 3.2 versus 3.7 means
codex_prompt_template: Your response must be either ‘true’ or ‘false’. Do not output any other text.

name: Blind Human Preference Test
one_liner: A blind comparison method where AI and human outputs are judged by human raters who don't know the source, using preference voting.
definition: An evaluation approach in which AI-generated and human-generated responses to the same task are presented to human evaluators without revealing which is which, and evaluators choose the preferred response. The percentage of votes indicates relative quality.
explanation: The actual evaluation poll was run by Lenny tweeting screenshots of two solutions, without saying which one was AI. People were encouraged to vote on which they thought was the better answer, and also tell us which one they thought was made by AI. We ran this as a blind test because that’s the only fair way to evaluate performance. Think of it like the Coke vs. Pepsi challenge: when Pepsi is in unmarked containers, people prefer the taste, but when people see the Coca-Cola brand on the can, they prefer that instead.
when_to_use: When comparing subjective quality of AI outputs against human baselines
When there is no ground truth and quality is in the eye of the beholder
To gauge user acceptance of AI-generated content
When evaluating subjective qualities like helpfulness, tone, or creativity
For tasks where there is no single correct answer
When you need to ensure user satisfaction improves
when_not_to_use: When objective metrics exist (e.g., accuracy, F1)
When the evaluation requires domain expertise that the general crowd lacks
When there is a risk of data contamination if results are public
When the task has clear objective standards that can be automated
When human evaluation is too expensive or slow for the development cycle
For early-stage prototyping where quick iteration is more important
common_pitfalls: Public voting leads to groupthink and bandwagon effects
Difficulty finding strong human baselines
Small sample sizes may lead to inconclusive results
Participants may guess the AI and be biased for or against it
Responses may be judged on style rather than substance
Inter-rater variability can skew results
Cost and time to recruit evaluators
Bias from interface or presentation order

name: Code-Based Eval
one_liner: Use deterministic code to check for simple, well-defined failure modes in AI outputs.
definition: An automated evaluator that uses a Python function or piece of code to check whether a specific failure mode is present in a trace, without using an LLM.
explanation: As Shreya explains, 'maybe I could write a Python function or a piece of code to check whether that failure mode is present in a trace or not. And that's possible to do for certain things like checking the output is JSON, or checking that it's markdown, or checking that it's short.' This approach is cheaper and faster than LLM-based evals and should be attempted first when the failure mode is deterministically detectable.
when_to_use: When the failure mode can be deterministically checked with code (e.g., output format, length, presence of certain strings)
When you need a fast, cheap evaluation that can run frequently
When evaluating deterministic criteria like code validity
When checking for presence of specific text strings
When fast, cheap evaluation is needed
When the failure mode is objective and rule-based
In CI pipelines to catch regressions quickly
For verifying structural properties of outputs
when_not_to_use: When the failure mode requires subjective judgment or semantic understanding
When the output is free-form and cannot be captured by simple rules
For subjective or open-ended quality assessment
When nuance or context interpretation is required
For subjective qualities like tone, relevance, or reasoning
When the rule cannot be easily encoded as code (e.g., semantic understanding)
When multiple outputs could be equally valid
For creative or open-ended tasks
metric_or_method: The method is to write a function that returns a boolean or a score based on predefined rules.
common_pitfalls: Over-reliance on simple checks for complex outputs
Misses nuances that human judges would catch
Trying to encode subjective judgment in rigid rules
Overlooking edge cases that break the code check
Not maintaining code checks as product evolves
Overly strict matching may reject acceptable variations
Does not capture semantic equivalence
Requires careful curation of test cases to avoid ambiguity

name: Continuous Calibration, Continuous Development (CCCD)
one_liner: An iterative AI product development framework that balances pre-deployment evaluation with post-deployment behavior calibration to safely increase an AI agent's autonomy over time.
definition: CCCD is a framework for building AI products that consists of two loops: Continuous Development (scope capability, curate data, design evaluation metrics, deploy) and Continuous Calibration (analyze production behavior, spot error patterns, apply fixes, design new evaluation metrics). It emphasizes starting with low-agency, high-control versions and gradually increasing autonomy as the system learns from real user interactions.
explanation: The CCCD framework addresses the challenge of unpredictable user behavior and emerging error patterns in AI agents. Instead of attempting full autonomy from day one, teams scope a minimal viable capability, curate a dataset of expected inputs/outputs, and design targeted evaluation metrics. After deployment, they monitor behavior, identify unexpected errors, and create new evaluation metrics specifically for those patterns. This cycle repeats, with each version increasing the agent's decision-making ability while building a flywheel of learnings. The approach ensures that evaluation metrics evolve with the product, and it prevents the common pitfall of having metrics that only cover pre-conceived errors.
when_to_use: Building complex AI agents with high decision autonomy (e.g., customer support, code review)
Enterprises with messy, undocumented workflows and data taxonomies
When you need to maintain customer trust and minimize risk of harmful autonomous actions
When you want to systematically learn from user behavior to improve your product
when_not_to_use: Simple, deterministic models with well-defined inputs/outputs
When you cannot instrument logging or production monitoring
For one-off prototypes or experiments where rapid iteration without monitoring is acceptable
When the cost of human-in-the-loop is prohibitive and full automation is the only viable option
metric_or_method: The method is the dual-loop process: development loop with curated evaluation datasets and defined evaluation metrics; calibration loop with behavioral analysis, error pattern detection, and metric expansion. Metrics are specific to the version and expected behaviors.
common_pitfalls: Skipping low-agency stages and attempting full autonomy immediately, leading to overwhelming error patterns
Expecting a single set of evaluation metrics to catch all issues without updating them from production learnings
Not aligning the team (PMs, engineers, domain experts) on expected behavior before curation
Over-investing in LLM judges that may not capture emerging error patterns
source_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
source_excerpt: The idea is pretty simple, which is we have this right side of the loop, which is continuous development, where you scope capability and curate data, essentially get a data set of what your expected inputs are and what your expected outputs should be looking at. [...] And then you set up the application and then design the right kind of evaluation metrics. [...] And the second part is the continuous calibration, which is the part where you understand what behavior you hadn't expected in the beginning [...] you analyze your behavior, spot error patterns, apply fixes for issues that you see, but you also design newer evaluation metrics to figure out that they are emerging patterns. [...] But what we specifically also mention is while you're going through these iterations, try to think of lower agency iterations in the beginning and higher control iterations. [...] The issue with just building a bunch of evaluation metrics and then having them in production is evaluation metrics catch only the errors that you're already aware of, but there can be a lot of emerging patterns that you understand only after you put things in production.
codex_prompt_template:
When building an AI agent, apply the CCCD framework:
1. Scope the initial capability and curate a dataset of expected inputs and ideal outputs.
2. Design specific evaluation metrics based on the expected behavior.
3. Deploy the minimal viable version with low agency and human oversight.
4. Monitor production behavior: log interactions, collect implicit/explicit signals.
5. Analyze error patterns that were not covered by initial metrics.
6. Create new evaluation metrics targeting those patterns, and apply fixes.
7. Gradually increase the agent's autonomy (e.g., from routing → suggestions → full resolution) as error rates drop and metrics show reliability.
8. Repeat the calibration cycle continuously.

name: DIY Data Inspection Dashboards
one_liner: Building simple, bespoke tools to make manual review and annotation of AI traces frictionless, accelerating error analysis.
definition: Creating lightweight internal applications that display your AI's interactions in structured formats, hiding complexity and surfacing relevant metadata, to enable rapid error spotting and labeling.
explanation: The highest-ROI activity in improving AI products is looking at raw data. To make this habit easy, teams can quickly build custom UIs using AI coding assistants. These tools hide distracting details (e.g., system prompts), provide quick annotation buttons, and organize traces by channel or error type. This removes the friction of slogging through logs and spreadsheets.
when_to_use: When you need to review a large number of traces regularly
When your team lacks a centralized way to inspect outputs
When you want to encourage frequent data engagement across the team
when_not_to_use: When you have only a handful of traces that can be reviewed in a spreadsheet
If you already have a robust commercial tool that meets your annotation needs
common_pitfalls: Over-engineering the tool before validating its usefulness
Building a general-purpose tool instead of one tailored to your specific data format
Neglecting user experience, making the tool tedious to use

name: Dual-Purpose Evaluations
one_liner: Distinguishing between evaluations used to train models and those used to benchmark and select model checkpoints.
definition: The practice of using evaluations both as training signals (rewarding good performance) and as benchmarks to measure progress and choose the best model version for release.
explanation: Evaluations serve a dual role: during training, they provide feedback to improve the model; after training, they enable objective comparison of different checkpoints. Recognizing this distinction helps in designing evaluation pipelines that avoid overfitting and ensure reliable progress tracking.
when_to_use: When building an end-to-end model development pipeline
To clarify the purpose of different evaluation sets
To prevent mixing training signals with final model selection criteria
common_pitfalls: Using the same evaluations for both training and checkpoint selection can cause overfitting and inflated performance metrics
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
source_excerpt: evals often covers two terms. One is you are using the evaluations for training because you're evaluating whether or not the model did a good job, and when it does do a good job, you're rewarding it. And then there's this other notion of evals where you're trying to measure the model's progress like, okay, yeah, I have five different candidate checkpoints and I want to pick the one that's best in order to release it to the public.

name: Error Analysis (Open/Axial Coding)
one_liner: A manual, domain-expert-led review of AI application traces to tag the first upstream error, providing a qualitative foundation for building evals.
definition: A qualitative evaluation technique where a domain expert inspects a sample of AI application traces, writing a brief free‑form note on the first upstream error they encounter, and continues until they reach theoretical saturation (no new error types are discovered).
explanation: Instead of jumping to automated tests, start by looking at actual user interactions in an observability tool. Go trace by trace, spot the earliest thing that goes wrong, jot a short note (like 'should have handed off to a human'), and move on. This low-ceremony process rapidly surfaces failure modes and grounds later metrics in real data. It works best when a single trusted domain expert (a 'benevolent dictator') does the tagging, not a committee, and stops once new errors stop appearing.
when_to_use: At the beginning of building an AI product to discover failure modes
When you need to ground your eval metrics with real user behavior
Whenever you have access to trace logs from your application
When you are unsure what to measure in an AI product
Before building any automated evaluation suite
When you need to align team on product quality criteria
Before creating any automated evals
When you are uncertain about failure modes
when_not_to_use: When you already have well-defined, automated evaluation metrics
When you need continuous, hands-off evaluation at scale
When no domain expert is available to review the traces
When you already have a robust, validated taxonomy of failures from prior analysis
For minor products where qualitative analysis is overkill
When you have a mature system with well-understood failure modes
In the very early stages of building if you have no production data yet
If you already have a high-quality labeled dataset and clear evaluation metrics.
common_pitfalls: Trying to automate this step with an LLM – it lacks the product context and will miss domain‑specific errors
Forming a committee and getting paralyzed by consensus
Attempting to capture every error in a trace instead of just the first upstream issue
Stopping too early (before saturation) or continuing long after no new insights appear
Starting with ready-made metrics like hallucination scores
Over-automating the coding process with LLMs without human review
Creating too many failure categories (aim for <10)
Skipping this step and jumping straight to writing evals based on assumptions
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
source_excerpt: The first step in conquering data like this is just to write notes. [...] So you would just write a quick note here. You would say, 'Okay.' You might pop in here, and you can write a note. [...] And the answer is, just write down the first thing that you see that's wrong, the most upstream error. Don't worry about all the errors, just capture the first thing that you see that's wrong, and stop, and move on. [...] Keep looking at traces until you feel like you're not learning anything new. [...] There's actually a term in data analysis and qualitative analysis called theoretical saturation. [...] when do you stop? It's when you are theoretically saturating or you're not uncovering any new types of notes, new types of concepts, or nothing that will materially change the next part of your process.
codex_prompt_template: You are an AI evaluation assistant. Review the provided application trace. Identify the first upstream error (the earliest thing that goes wrong). Write a short, free‑style note describing the error. If no error is apparent, respond with 'OK'.

name: Eval-Driven Improvement Flywheel
one_liner: Post‑deployment loop of running evals on live data, analyzing error patterns manually, and applying fixes to iteratively improve the system.
definition: The calibration phase where teams assess system performance on real user interactions, identify recurring error patterns through manual review, and refine both the system and evaluation metrics based on observed behavior.
explanation: After deployment, you continuously run your evaluation metrics on live interaction data (smartly sampled using signals like control handoffs). You then manually review the weakest areas to spot recurring error patterns, document them, and drive targeted fixes. This loop often reveals that the original evals were themselves misaligned with real usage, so they may need to be redesigned – a normal and necessary part of the process.
when_to_use: After deploying an AI product to users
When you have live interaction logs
When you need to iteratively improve system performance and increase agency
When you have a live AI product with monitoring and CI in place
To institutionalize continuous improvement
To ensure that once a failure is discovered, it's permanently fixed
when_not_to_use: Before any deployment
When the system has no live usage
When the problem is purely deterministic and does not require iterative calibration
Before you have built evaluators and CI
For products with infrequent updates or low impact failures
metric_or_method: Custom eval metrics, live data sampling, manual error pattern analysis, and iterative eval refinement.
common_pitfalls: Skipping manual error analysis and jumping directly to fixes
Not updating evaluation metrics when real‑world behavior diverges from the reference
Overfitting to early user behavior without testing on broader populations
Not closing the loop by updating CI golden datasets with new failures
Treating flywheel as a one-time setup instead of an ongoing process
Ignoring the need for human domain expertise in analysis
source_title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
source_excerpt: Once you’ve collected a meaningful amount of live interaction data, you can start running your evaluation. [...] Now it’s time to start manually reviewing your data. [...] you’ll start to notice repeat error patterns. [...] So step 6 may also involve revisiting and rebuilding your evals—and that’s completely normal.
codex_prompt_template:
Given a set of live interaction data and evaluation metrics, perform the following steps:
1. Run metrics on the data, focusing on subsets with the lowest scores.
2. Sample 20‑50 low‑scoring examples for manual review.
3. Identify recurring error patterns and categorize them.
4. Propose targeted fixes or architectural changes.
5. Re‑evaluate after applying fixes, and if the metrics show consistent blind spots, redesign the evaluation metrics themselves.

name: Evals
one_liner: Structured ways to measure how well an AI system performs on specific tasks, such as correctness, safety, helpfulness, or tone.
definition: Structured ways to measure how well an AI system performs on specific tasks, such as correctness, safety, helpfulness, or tone.
explanation: Evals (short for “evaluations”) define what “good” looks like for your AI system and help you answer the question: Is this model doing what I want it to do? They are basically unit tests or benchmarks for your AI product. They run your model through predefined inputs and compare its responses against expected outputs. This helps you quantify progress, catch regressions, and guide improvements.
when_to_use: When you need to quantify progress of your AI system.
When you need to catch regressions after model or prompt changes.
When you want to guide improvements in correctness, safety, helpfulness, or tone.
metric_or_method: Comparison of model responses against expected outputs for predefined inputs.
source_title: An AI glossary
source_excerpt:
Evals (short for “evaluations”) are structured ways to measure how well an AI system performs on specific tasks, such as correctness, safety, helpfulness, or tone. They define what “good” looks like for your AI system and help you answer the question: Is this model doing what I want it to do?

Evals are basically unit tests or benchmarks for your AI product. They run your model through predefined inputs and compare its responses against expected outputs. This helps you quantify progress, catch regressions, and guide you toward improvements.

For example, here’s what an eval to measure the toxicity and tone of a model’s response might look like. Your model output would be inserted into the {text} variable:
codex_prompt_template: Evaluate the following AI-generated text for toxicity and tone on a scale of 1 to 5: {text}

name: Expert Human Evaluation
one_liner: Use expert human annotators to deeply evaluate model outputs on real-world tasks across multiple dimensions.
definition: A method where domain experts interact with AI models on relevant tasks and evaluate outputs thoroughly for accuracy, usefulness, and nuanced quality, beyond superficial checks.
explanation: Instead of relying on benchmarks or casual user votes, hire experts (e.g., Nobel laureates, teachers, professional coders) to have substantive conversations with the model and assess its responses by actually working through them. They evaluate dimensions like accuracy, instruction following, and depth. Contrast with vibe-based voting.
when_to_use: To get accurate measure of model performance on real-world, ambiguous tasks
When high-stakes evaluation is needed (e.g., safety, factual accuracy)
When tasks require deep domain expertise
when_not_to_use: When quick, cheap feedback is sufficient
When tasks have objective ground truth that can be automated
When scalability and speed are primary concerns
common_pitfalls: Expensive and slow to run at scale
Requires careful expert selection and task design
May still introduce subjectivity or annotator bias
Difficult to standardize across different domains
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
source_excerpt: the way we really care about measuring model progress is by running all these human evaluations. [...] we will take Gore human annotators, and we'll ask them, 'Okay, go have a conversational model.' [...] And because or searchers or annotators, they are experts at the top of their fields, and they are not just giving your responses, they're actually working through the responses deeply themselves [...] they're going to evaluate the code that it write. They're going to double check the physics equations that it writes. They're going to evaluate the models in a very deep way, so they're going to pay attention to accuracy and instruction following, all these things that casual users don't [...] I think that's a much better approach than these benchmarks or these random outline AV tests.
codex_prompt_template: You are an expert in {domain}. Please interact with the AI system as you would in your daily work. Then assess the quality of its responses on the following axes: accuracy, relevance, depth, and adherence to instructions. Provide a detailed justification for each rating.

name: Four-Part Eval Formula
one_liner: A structured prompt template for LLM-based evals: role, context, goal, terminology/label.
definition: A formula for writing LLM eval prompts consisting of four distinct parts: setting the role, providing the context, providing the goal, and defining terminology and labels.
explanation: Ensures clarity and specificity in evaluation, translating user expectations into precise criteria for the judge LLM.
when_to_use: When writing LLM-based eval prompts
To standardize eval construction across a team
When precision and consistency are critical
when_not_to_use: For code-based or human evals that don't use LLM prompts
When evaluation task is too simple for structured breakdown
common_pitfalls: Skipping the role or label definition, leading to ambiguous results
Not iterating on the prompt after initial failures
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
source_excerpt: Each great LLM eval contains four distinct parts: - **Part 1:** **Setting the role.** You need to provide the judge-LLM a role (e.g. “you are examining written text”) so that the system is primed for the task. - **Part 2: Providing the context.** This is the data you will actually be sending to the LLM to grade. This will come from your application (i.e. the message chain, or the message generated from the agent LLM). - **Part 3: Providing the goal.** Clearly articulating what you want your judge-LLM to measure isn’t just a step in the process; it’s the difference between a mediocre AI and one that consistently delights users. [...] You need to clearly define what success and failure look like to the judge-LLM [...] - **Part 4: Defining the terminology and label.** Toxicity, for example, can mean different things in different contexts. You want to be specific here so the judge-LLM is “grounded” in the terminology you care about.

name: Golden Dataset for CI Regression Testing
one_liner: A curated set of examples used as a regression test suite in CI to prevent reintroduced AI failures.
definition: The golden dataset is a purpose-built stress test containing examples covering core features, edge cases, and regression tests for every fixed bug, run automatically on each change.
explanation: Integrated into CI, code-based evaluators check outputs against this dataset. A passing build means no known failures have regressed. It's a safety net, not a quality guarantee; it ensures stability.
when_to_use: After you have identified and fixed specific failures
To prevent backsliding in production
When you have CI infrastructure
when_not_to_use: As a substitute for production monitoring
When you haven't yet built code-based evaluators
If the dataset is not maintained and becomes stale
common_pitfalls: Assuming a passing CI build means high production quality
Not updating the dataset with new failure modes
Using only trivial examples that don't stress the system
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: The foundation of CI for AI is a golden dataset, a curated collection of examples that represent the most important scenarios for your product... On every code or prompt change, your CI pipeline runs the system against this golden dataset and checks the outputs with your fast, deterministic, code-based evaluators.
codex_prompt_template: N/A

name: Guardrail Evaluators
one_liner: Synchronous, low-latency checks that run in the request path to block or redact harmful outputs before reaching the user.
definition: Guardrails are fast, deterministic checks—like regexes, blocklists, or schema validators—that operate synchronously to prevent critical failures in real time.
explanation: They are used for high-impact failures where prevention is essential. Must have very low false-positive rate to avoid blocking valid responses. In rare cases, an LLM-as-a-judge can be a guardrail if latency allows.
when_to_use: For critical failures that must never reach the user (e.g., medical advice, safety)
When you need real-time intervention
When the check can be done quickly and deterministically
when_not_to_use: For non-critical issues where latency or false positives are too costly
When the check requires complex subjective judgment that would take too long
common_pitfalls: High false-positive rate causing production bugs
Using slow LLM judges inline without considering latency
Not logging guardrail triggers for monitoring
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: For critical, high-impact failures, you can use a special type of synchronous evaluator called a guardrail. These run in the request path and can block, redact, or regenerate a response before the user ever sees it. Most guardrails are fast, deterministic checks... Crucially, they must have a very low false-positive rate.

For factual Q&A applications

when_not_to_use: For purely creative tasks where factual grounding is not required
When no reference context is available
common_pitfalls: Assuming all plausible-sounding output is correct
Not testing on diverse document types

Useful for: When you are providing documents (e.g. PDFs) for the agent to perform reasoning on top of

name: Hero Use Case Eval Loop
one_liner: A process of defining high-value user scenarios with ideal outputs and creating evaluation benchmarks to iteratively improve model performance.
definition: A methodology where product teams identify critical user scenarios (hero use cases), define gold-standard responses, and convert them into evaluation tests that drive iterative model fine-tuning and product development.
explanation: When building an AI product, you start by defining the most important use cases you want the model to handle well. For each, you specify what an ideal output looks like. These become evaluation metrics (evals). As you fine-tune the model, you measure performance on these evals. If scores improve, you have confidence the product is becoming effective. This ties product design directly to model performance.
when_to_use: When building a new AI feature where quality on specific tasks is crucial
When you need to measure progress during model fine-tuning
When you want to align model improvement with product goals
when_not_to_use: When use cases are not well-defined or too broad
When the model is already performing adequately for the task
When you don't have resources to create gold-standard answers
metric_or_method: Methodology: Create a set of question-answer pairs based on hero use cases and track accuracy or quality metrics.
common_pitfalls: Overfitting evals to specific examples, leading to poor generalization
Spending too much time on evals without iterating on the model
Not updating evals as product requirements evolve
source_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)
source_excerpt: As we were building that product, we were designing evals at the same time as we were thinking about how this product was going to work and we were trying to go through hero use cases. Here's a question you want to be able to ask. Here's an amazing answer for that question. And then turning those into evals and then hill climbing on those evals. [...] And so as we were fine-tuning our model for deep research to be able to answer these things, we were able to test is it getting better on these evals that we said were important measures of how the product was working? And it's when you start seeing that and you start seeing performance on evals going up, you start saying, 'Okay, I think we have a product here.'
codex_prompt_template: Write an eval set for the following hero use case: [Use case description]. For each, include the exact input query and the expected ideal output. Ensure the queries cover both straightforward and edge cases.

name: Human Evals
one_liner: Collect human feedback to evaluate LLM outputs, using end-user ratings or subject-matter expert labeling.
definition: Human evals are human feedback loops designed into the product, such as thumbs-up/down or comment boxes, or using human labelers to provide labels and feedback.
explanation: Directly ties evaluation to end-user satisfaction but can be sparse, noisy, and costly if hiring labelers.
when_to_use: When user satisfaction is paramount
When subjective assessment is required
When aligning with human preferences via RLHF
when_not_to_use: When fast, scalable evaluation is needed
When cost is a constraint
When the task is objective and automatable
common_pitfalls: Sparse signal due to low user engagement with feedback mechanisms
Ambiguity in simple ratings like thumbs-up/down
High cost for expert labeling
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
source_excerpt: 1. **Human evals:** These are human feedback loops you can design into your product (i.e. showing a thumbs-up/thumbs-down or a comment box next to an LLM response, for your user to provide feedback). You can also have human labelers (i.e. subject-matter experts) provide their labels and feedback, and use this for aligning the application with human preferences via prompt optimization or fine-tuning a model (aka reinforcement learning from human feedback, or RLHF). - **Pro:** Directly tied to the end user. - **Cons:** Very sparse (most people don’t hit that thumbs-up/thumbs-down), not a strong signal (what does a thumbs-up or -down mean?), and costly (if you want to hire human labelers).

name: Iterative Eval Development Workflow
one_liner: A four-phase process: Collection, First-pass evaluation, Iteration loop, Production monitoring.
definition: An iterative workflow for building and refining evals, from gathering data to continuous monitoring in production.
explanation: Emphasizes continuous improvement: collect real-world interactions, run initial evals, refine prompts and data, and monitor live performance.
when_to_use: When developing a new eval from scratch
When integrating evals into a CI/CD pipeline for AI
When systematic improvement of AI quality is needed
when_not_to_use: For one-off, ad-hoc manual checks
When there is no production environment to monitor
common_pitfalls: Making evals too complex too quickly
Not testing for edge cases
Forgetting to validate eval results against real user feedback

[...] **Phase 1: Collection** [...] **Phase 2: First-pass evaluation** [...] **Phase 3: Iteration loop** [...] **Phase 4: Production monitoring** [...] Common mistakes I’ve seen teams make when adopting evals: 1. Making evals too complex too quickly [...] 2. Not testing for edge cases. [...] 3. Forgetting to validate eval results against real user feedback.

name: Judge Alignment via Confusion Matrix
one_liner: Validate an LLM judge by comparing its binary judgments against human labels using a confusion matrix, and iterate to minimize both false positives and false negatives.
definition: The process of ensuring an LLM‑as‑a‑Judge produces judgments that align with human assessors, by measuring agreement on a labeled dataset and analyzing mismatches through a confusion matrix (or pivot table), then refining the judge prompt to reduce discrepancies.
explanation: Hamel warns against trusting a judge without validation: 'before you release your LLM as a judge, you want to make sure it's aligned to the human... you want to measure your judge against the axial code... Measure the agreement.' He stresses that overall agreement can be misleading if errors are rare, so you must examine off‑diagonal cells: 'you need to look at each type of error. When the human said false, but the judge said true, or vice versa... iterate on your prompt... reduce misalignment.' Shreya adds that skipping this step is a 'bad smell'.
when_to_use: After creating an LLM judge prompt and before deploying it in production or CI
Whenever you need to trust the automated evaluation metrics
when_not_to_use: When you don't have human‑labeled data for the specific failure mode (but you should collect it)
When the cost of human labeling is prohibitive for the initial alignment (though a small set may suffice)
metric_or_method: Create a labeled dataset with human judgments. Run the LLM judge on the same data. Construct a confusion matrix (e.g., a pivot table with rows = human judgment, columns = judge judgment) and measure true/false positives/negatives. Iterate on the judge prompt to reduce off‑diagonal counts.
common_pitfalls: Relying solely on overall agreement percentage (e.g., 90% accuracy) when the failure mode is rare, because a judge that always says ‘pass’ could achieve high agreement.
Not iterating on the prompt after identifying misalignment, accepting mediocre alignment.
Not looking at both types of error (false positives and false negatives) and only optimizing for one.
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
source_excerpt: a lot of people go straight to this agreement. They say, 'Okay, my judge agrees with the human some percentage of the time.' Now that sounds appealing, but it's a very dangerous metric to use, because a lot of times, errors, they only happen on the long tail and they don't happen as frequently, so if you only have the error 10% of the time, then you can easily have 90% agreement by just having a judge say it passes all the time. [...] you need to look at each type of error. When the human said false, but the judge said true, or vice versa, so those non‑green diagonals here, and if they're too large, then go iterate on your prompt, make it more clear to the LLM judge, so that you can reduce that misalignment.

name: LLM Arena Voting
one_liner: Crowd-sourced pairwise comparison where users vote on AI responses based on superficial impressions.
definition: An evaluation method where an online leaderboard displays two anonymized model responses side-by-side and asks users to vote for the better one, aggregating votes to rank models.
explanation: As described, users quickly skim responses and pick the one that looks flashier (emojis, bolding, length) without fact-checking, leading to models optimizing for engagement and appearance rather than truth. Highly susceptible to superficial manipulation.
when_to_use: For comparing model preferences at scale with low cost
For gathering human feedback on subjective tasks where surface appeal matters
When a quick, high-level leaderboard is needed for marketing
when_not_to_use: When accuracy, factuality, or safety are critical
When deep domain expertise is needed
When models can game superficial features (e.g., by adding emojis, bold text)
common_pitfalls: Voters are not representative and only skim responses
Incentivizes flashy but incorrect responses
Easily manipulated by superficial output formatting
Does not measure factual correctness or utility
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
source_excerpt: So right now, the industry is played by these terrible databoards like LLM Arena. It's this popular online leaderboard where random people from around the world vote on which AI response is better. But the thing is, like I was saying earlier, they're not carefully reading or fact-checking. They're skimming these responses for two seconds and picking whatever looks flashiest. [...] The easiest way to climb LLM Arena, it's adding crazy boating. It's doubling the number of emojis. It's tripling the length of your model responses, even if your model starts hallucinating and getting the answer completely wrong.
codex_prompt_template: You are a user evaluating two anonymized AI assistant responses. Select the one you find more helpful, informative, and well-presented. Do not fact-check; go with your first impression.

name: LLM-as-a-Judge
one_liner: Use an LLM to evaluate complex, subjective failure modes in AI outputs, acting as a binary judge for a specific, scoped criterion.
definition: An automated evaluator that uses an LLM to assess whether a specific, narrow failure mode is present in an AI output, typically outputting a pass/fail judgment.
explanation: LLM as a judge is a meta‑eval that tackles issues too nuanced for code, like 'human handoff errors' or 'conversational flow issues.' As Shreya notes, 'you're asking the judge to do one thing, evaluate one failure mode, so the scope of the problem is very small and the output of this LLM judge is pass or fail.' Hamel adds that you must 'eval that eval to make sure the LLM that's judging is doing the right thing' by aligning it with human labels.
when_to_use: When the failure mode is too complex for code‑based checks (e.g., 'conversational flow', 'human handoff errors')
When you need automated, scalable evaluation of subjective quality issues
When you have labeled examples to align the judge against
When scalable, automated evaluation is needed
When human-like judgment at volume is required
When explainability in evaluation results is desired
For subjective failure modes like tone, relevance, empathy, or reasoning quality
When you need to scale evaluation without sacrificing quality bar alignment
when_not_to_use: When you can use a simpler, code‑based check
When you cannot afford the cost or latency of LLM calls for evaluation
When the failure mode is extremely rare and you don't have enough labeled data to validate the judge
When deterministic outcomes are required
When there is very limited data to validate the judge
When LLM inference cost is prohibitive
For objective failures that can be handled by code-based evaluators
When latency or cost of LLM calls is prohibitive for the use case
metric_or_method: Craft a prompt that instructs the LLM to output a binary true/false based on specific rules describing the failure mode, then validate against human labels.
common_pitfalls: Accepting the LLM judge's output as ground truth without validating against human judgments
Using rating scales (1‑5) instead of binary decisions, leading to ambiguous metrics
Not iterating on the prompt to reduce misalignment with human labels
Over‑relying on overall agreement percentage, which can be misleading if errors are rare
Probabilistic results require sufficient volume to trust the signal
Initial calibration needed with labeled examples
Using Likert scales instead of binary pass/fail
Not splitting data, leading to overfitting on the dev set
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
source_excerpt: 3. **LLM-based evals:** This technique utilizes an external LLM system (i.e. a “judge” LLM), with a prompt like the one above, to grade the output of the agent system. LLM-based evals allow you to generate classification labels in an automated way that resembles human-labeled data—without needing to have users or subject-matter experts label all of your data. - **Pro:** Highly scalable (it’s like a human labeling your data but much cheaper) and uses natural language, so PMs can write prompts directly. You can get the LLM to generate explanations for its judgments, making them more reliable and explainable for debugging. While individual judgments may be subjective, they become empirically useful over large datasets—if a human can grade something, so can an LLM. Production systems often use techniques like confidence scores or panels of LLM judges to increase reliability. - **Con:** Requires initial setup of the LLM-as-judge system with some labeled examples to validate performance. Results are probabilistic rather than deterministic, so you need sufficient volume to trust the signal.

You are an evaluator. Assess the following LLM output for a specific failure mode. Return true if the failure mode is present, false otherwise. 

Failure mode description: [DESCRIBE FAILURE MODE AND RULES]

Output:
```
[LLM OUTPUT]
```

Return only 'true' or 'false'.

sources.title: Five proven prompt engineering techniques (and a few more-advanced tactics)
sources.excerpt: Human feedback is expensive and slow, so it’s common practice among AI practitioners to use an AI model to evaluate and rate the quality of outputs, either from itself or from other sources. This method leverages the AI’s analytical capabilities to provide a more objective assessment of content quality. It can be particularly useful for tasks like content moderation, quality control, or comparing multiple AI-generated outputs to select the best one. However, it’s important to remember that the AI’s judgments are based on its training and may not always align perfectly with human assessments.

name: LLM Introspection Debugging
one_liner: Ask the model to explain its own unexpected actions to uncover hidden flaws in instructions or system design.
definition: A debugging approach where, upon observing an unexpected model behavior, you prompt the model to reflect on its decision-making process, often revealing ambiguities in the prompt, tool delegation errors, or missing contextual cues.
explanation: Cat Wu describes instances where the model takes an action like running tests without using the UI. By asking the model to reflect, she discovered issues like confusing system prompts or subagent failures. This curiosity-driven method helps patch the 'harness' around the model.
when_to_use: When you encounter a surprising model failure that isn't clearly due to a known limitation
When you want to improve prompt/system design based on model's own 'reasoning'
When debugging complex agentic behavior chains
when_not_to_use: When the error is obvious and doesn't need introspection (e.g., a syntax error)
When the model's self-explanations are known to be hallucinated or unreliable
When you need a systematic analysis rather than ad-hoc debugging
metric_or_method: Prompt the model with a reflective question like 'Why did you do X?' or 'Reflect on your last action; what led to this?' and analyze its response to identify the root cause.
common_pitfalls: The model might invent plausible but false explanations (hallucination)
Over-relying on introspection without verifying with actual data
Assuming the model's self-diagnosis is always correct without cross-checking the system prompt or code
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
source_excerpt: Sometimes when I notice that the model does something unexpected, like for example, there's situations where the model will make a front end change and run tests, but not actually use the UI. It's actually pretty useful to ask the model to reflect on why I did this, and sometimes they'll say that, 'Hey, there was something confusing in the system prompt,' or, 'I didn't realize that the front end verification was part of this task,' Or, 'Hey, I delegated the verification to this subagent and the subagent didn't do the test and I didn't check its work.' A lot of times just being very curious about why the model made the decision that it did will show you what misled it so that you can fix the harness in order to close this gap.
codex_prompt_template: After an unexpected action, ask the model: 'Please reflect on why you [action]. What in your instructions or understanding led to this, and how could I clarify the task to avoid this in the future?'

name: Off-the-Shelf Metric Mining
one_liner: Using pre-built metrics like hallucination scores to sort traces and uncover surprising failure modes or successes.
definition: Calculate off-the-shelf metrics (hallucination, toxicity, etc.) on traces, then review those with extreme scores to discover patterns.
explanation: Instead of displaying generic metric scores on a dashboard, this technique uses them as a diagnostic tool. By sorting traces by high/low scores, you can find unexpected behaviors that inform custom evaluator design. It's an advanced method to be used after mastering basic error analysis.
when_to_use: After you have established a baseline understanding of your product's failures via manual error analysis
When you want to explore large datasets for hidden patterns
As a supplementary discovery method
when_not_to_use: As a replacement for targeted custom evaluators
When the off-the-shelf metric is known to be unreliable for your domain
If you lack capacity for human review of flagged examples
common_pitfalls: Paying attention to off-the-shelf scores directly without human review
Assuming these metrics correlate with actual product quality
Over-reliance on automated sorting without domain expertise
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: While off-the-shelf metrics like hallucination and toxicity are not worth paying attention to directly, they can be used in creative ways. Instead of reporting a hallucination or toxicity score on a dashboard, calculate the scores on your traces and sort them by high/low score. Reviewing the highest and lowest scoring examples can reveal surprising failure modes or unexpected successes...

name: Pesky-Failure-First Eval Selection
one_liner: Focus evaluation efforts only on the most persistent and high-risk failure modes that survive prompt fixes.
definition: A strategy where, after error analysis, you first address failures by improving the system prompt, and then build targeted evals only for the remaining stubborn issues that are critical to business or user experience.
explanation: Not every failure needs a dedicated eval. Many issues disappear once you update the prompt to clarify desired behavior. Reserve your limited engineering resources for the 'pesky' problems that keep recurring despite prompt refinements, especially those with high user or business impact (e.g., safety violations, critical handoff failures). This maximizes the ROI of your eval efforts.
when_to_use: After completing error analysis and fixing obvious prompt deficiencies
When resources are limited and you cannot create evals for all known issues
To safeguard against the highest-severity regressions
when_not_to_use: Before you have explored actual failure modes
When you have ample resources and can afford comprehensive evals
For issues that can be fully resolved with simple prompt changes
common_pitfalls: Spending time writing evals for issues that are easily fixed in the prompt
Neglecting high-risk but low-frequency failure modes
Assuming that all failures require an automated eval

source_excerpt: It's not that many, because a lot of the failure modes, as Hamel said earlier, can be fixed by just fixing your prompt. You shouldn't do an eval like this for everything, just the pesky ones that you've described your ideal behavior in your agent prompt, but it's still failing. [...] the whole game here is about prioritizing. You have finite resources and finite time, you can't write an eval for everything, so prioritize the ones that are the more pesky areas.

name: Production Monitoring with Asynchronous Evaluators

explanation: Because LLM judges may be too slow for real-time, they are run asynchronously. Results are statistically corrected using judge error rates. This monitors quality over time and flags new issues for error analysis.

To detect drift or new failure modes

name: Reinforcement Learning Environments
one_liner: Simulated worlds that evaluate and train models on complex, multi-step tasks through reward-based feedback.
definition: A framework of simulated real-world environments where AI models are deployed to perform end-to-end tasks, interact with various tools and entities, and receive rewards based on their performance, serving both as evaluation benchmarks and training playgrounds.
explanation: These environments expose model weaknesses in real-world complexity: messy inputs, novel tools, long time horizons, and interdependent actions. By observing model behavior over entire trajectories, they highlight failures that isolated benchmarks miss and provide rich feedback for improvement.
when_to_use: To evaluate and improve model performance on complex, real-world tasks

To identify failure modes that only appear in integrated, dynamic scenarios
common_pitfalls: Models may reward-hack to achieve correct answers without proper reasoning
Evaluating only final outcomes ignores valuable process information in trajectories
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
source_excerpt: An RL environment is essentially a simulation of real world... we give them models tasks in these environments... then we run them to see how they perform. And then we teach them, we give them these rewards when they're doing a good job or a bad job. [...] these environments really showcase where models are weak at end-to-end tasks in real world.

name: Retriever/Generator Split Evaluation
one_liner: Independently evaluating the retriever with recall@k and the generator with faithfulness/answer relevance in RAG systems.
definition: An evaluation framework that separates RAG assessment into retrieval quality (recall@k) and generation quality (faithfulness to sources and relevance to query).
explanation: The retriever is treated as a search problem; recall@k measures if relevant docs are retrieved. Once retrieval is solid, the generator is evaluated on faithfulness (grounding) and answer relevance. Fix retriever first.
when_to_use: For any RAG-based AI product
When end-to-end correctness alone doesn't diagnose issues
To iteratively improve retrieval and generation separately
when_not_to_use: For non-RAG systems
When you don't have access to intermediate retrieval results
common_pitfalls: Using end-to-end accuracy as the sole metric
Ignoring recall@k in favor of precision only
Not tuning k appropriately based on query complexity
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: First, evaluate the retriever. Treat it as a search problem... The most critical metric for RAG is often recall@k... Once your retriever is performing well... you can evaluate the generator. Here you are primarily measuring two things. First is faithfulness... Second is answer relevance...
codex_prompt_template: N/A

name: Role-Specific Skill Taxonomy for AI Benchmarking
one_liner: A framework that decomposes a job role into a taxonomy of skills to systematically evaluate AI's automation potential through targeted task benchmarks.
definition: A structured approach where a professional role is broken down into categories and sub-skills, and AI is tested on representative real-world tasks for each skill component to estimate overall job replaceability.
explanation: Lenny defined a PM skills framework with categories like Shape the Product, Ship the Product, Sync the People, and sub-skills under each. By aligning benchmark tasks with this taxonomy, one can track what percentage of the PM role is automatable.
when_to_use: When evaluating AI's potential to replace or augment complex human roles
To create a comprehensive, structured benchmark for a profession
To identify which parts of a job are most susceptible to automation
when_not_to_use: When evaluating a narrow, single-function AI capability
When the role's tasks cannot be easily categorized
For quick, informal assessments
common_pitfalls: May not capture tacit, unwritten knowledge essential to the role
Sub-skills might oversimplify the interconnected nature of real-world work
Requires domain experts to define the taxonomy and source challenging tasks
Risk of data contamination if tasks and answers are made public
source_title: How close is AI to replacing product managers?
source_excerpt: Lenny once defined the job of a product manager as to “deliver business impact...”. He also defined a comprehensive framework for categorizing PM skills (below). Aligning the hard PM task benchmarks with this framework will help us track what percentage of the overall PM role is currently automatable.

name: Rubrics and Verifiers
one_liner: Human experts define scoring rubrics that serve as verifiers for scalable automated model evaluation and reinforcement learning.
definition: A method where domain experts create a detailed rubric of criteria for a task, which an AI system uses to automatically score model outputs, enabling scalable evaluation and reinforcement learning from AI feedback.
explanation: Instead of relying on human feedback for each model output, experts define success criteria (e.g., key points to cover in a legal contract redlining). This rubric acts as a verifier: an AI can apply it to score model responses, and these scores can be used both to evaluate model performance and to provide rewards in reinforcement learning, making the process more data-efficient and scalable.
when_to_use: When you need scalable, high-quality evaluation in complex domains requiring expert knowledge
When moving from RLHF to RLAIF to reduce human labor in the loop
When creating benchmarks that reflect real-world professional standards
When you need detailed, structured feedback on model outputs
For training models to improve on specific error types
When human preference ratings are insufficient for capturing nuanced correctness
when_not_to_use: Simple tasks where automated heuristics or unit tests suffice
Lack of access to domain experts for rubric creation
Early-stage prototyping where quick human feedback is more informative
metric_or_method: Human-defined rubric evaluated by AI verifier
source_title: Why experts writing AI evals is creating the fastest-growing companies in history | Brendan Foody (CEO of Mercor)
source_excerpt: What everyone is generally moving towards is reinforcement learning from AI feedback instead of human feedback where you have instead the human defined some sort of success criteria, some way to measure that. And examples in code, it could be a unit test. We can scalably measure success and other domains that could be a rubric. And then you use that to incentivize model capabilities.

sources.title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
sources.excerpt: rubrics and verifiers have become very important. And rubrics and verifiers are like learning by being graded and getting detailed feedback on where you went wrong.

name: Single-Turn Reproduction for Multi-turn Debugging
one_liner: Diagnosing conversational failures by reproducing the error in a single turn to isolate knowledge gaps from context loss.
definition: A debugging technique where you take a failing multi-turn interaction and try to reproduce the failure with a direct, single-turn query.
explanation: If the system still fails, the problem is likely a simple knowledge or retrieval issue. If it succeeds, the failure is conversational (context loss or misinterpretation). This saves time by quickly pinpointing the root cause.
when_to_use: When analyzing failures in multi-turn chatbots
To quickly narrow down the cause of a failure
Before committing to complex multi-turn evaluation strategies
when_not_to_use: For single-turn systems
When the failure is inherently multi-turn (e.g., sequence of actions required)
common_pitfalls: Assuming that because single-turn works, the multi-turn failure is purely contextual (it could still be a retrieval issue with specific phrasing)
Not considering that the single-turn prompt may need to mimic the conversational context exactly
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: When a conversation fails, the next step is to isolate the root cause. A common mistake is to assume the failure is due to the complexities of dialogue. Before you dive into multi-turn analysis, try to reproduce the failure in a single turn.
codex_prompt_template: N/A

name: Small-Batch Eval Building
one_liner: Build a small set of high-quality evals to define and measure product goals, rather than creating many superficial tests.
definition: A product development practice of creating a handful (e.g., 5-10) of carefully designed evaluations to quantify success criteria, track progress, and guide prompt improvements.
explanation: Cat Wu emphasizes that you don't need hundreds of evals; just 10 great ones can help the team quantify goals and identify gaps. She personally builds evals for features needing product definition, and then shares the evals, run instructions, success/failure results, and prompt adjustments to improve success rates.
when_to_use: When a feature's success criteria are unclear
When you need to measure progress concretely
When you want to drive prompt engineering iterations
When you want to define product goals for the team
when_not_to_use: When the feature already has well-established quantitative metrics
When the problem is purely about model capability and not prompt design
When the team is too early for evaluation because the feature is still being ideated
metric_or_method: Create 5-10 evals, run them, report pass/fail, and suggest prompt adjustments to increase success rate.
common_pitfalls: Building too many evals that are shallow and don't target key product behaviors
Not iterating on prompts based on eval results
Assuming that once evals pass, the product is complete, without further user testing
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
source_excerpt: Just building 10 great evals is important for helping the team quantify what the goal is and what their progress towards it is and what they're missing [...] I personally jump into evals when there's a feature that I think needs a bit more product definition, and often the output of this is, 'Okay, here are five evals that I made. This is how you run them. These are the ones that succeed and these are the ones that don't, and this is the prompt that I've used to increase the success rate.'
codex_prompt_template: I've built these evals for [feature]: [list of eval descriptions]. Run them by [instructions]. Results: [pass/fail list]. To improve, adjust the prompt to [specific change] and re-run.

name: Spreadsheet-Based Ideal Behavior Specification
one_liner: Articulate ideal behavior for use cases in a simple, spreadsheet-like format to communicate product expectations and create evaluation criteria.
definition: A product evaluation methodology where you outline clearly specified ideal model responses for various use cases, often in a spreadsheet, to define success and provide concrete training targets.
explanation: This pattern involves writing down exactly what the AI should do in given scenarios, essentially creating a specification of desired outputs. It serves as a bridge between product and research by translating product goals into concrete evaluation criteria that can be used to train and assess models. It demystifies 'evals' by framing them as nothing more than clearly documented expectations.
when_to_use: When building AI product features that require nuanced or context-dependent behavior.
When you need to align product vision with ML training objectives.
When you want to systematically test model behavior against real-world use cases.
When onboarding new team members to product expectations.
When introducing a new feature with specific behavioral requirements
When you need to align product expectations with model outputs
For early-stage prototyping and rapid iteration
when_not_to_use: When the model's behavior is fully covered by existing benchmarks and no customization is needed.
When the product is extremely simple with well-defined, unambiguous outputs.
When you lack clarity on what the ideal behavior should be.
For large-scale, continuous metric tracking
When the evaluators lack deep domain knowledge of the feature
When the behavior is too complex to capture in a simple spreadsheet
metric_or_method: Write clear, example-driven specifications of ideal model outputs for key use cases.
common_pitfalls: Overcomplicating the process by assuming it requires advanced tools or ML expertise.
Being too vague in the specifications, leading to ambiguous evaluation.
Focusing only on happy paths and neglecting edge cases.
Writing specs that are not actionable for training (e.g., too subjective).
May produce biased or limited coverage if only a few people contribute
Requires clear and consistent annotation guidelines
Static sheets can become outdated as the model or requirements evolve
source_title: Inside ChatGPT: The fastest-growing product in history | Nick Turley (Head of ChatGPT at OpenAI)
source_excerpt: I started writing evals before I knew what an eval was because I was just outlining very clearly specified ideal behavior for various use cases until someone told me, 'Hey, you should make an eval.' [...] And at the end of the day, it's not that different from the wisdom of, you ought to articulate success before you do anything else. It's just a new mechanism for doing that. But you can do it in a spreadsheet, you do it anywhere, and I really wanted to mystify it for people who hear that term. It's not some technical magic that you have to understand. It's really just about articulating success in a way that is maximally useful for training bots.
codex_prompt_template:
Write an evaluation specification for the following use case:

Use Case: [Describe the scenario]

Ideal Behavior:
- The model should [criterion 1]
- The model should [criterion 2]
- ...

Examples:
Input: [sample input]
Expected Output: [sample output]

Unacceptable Behavior:
- The model should NOT [criterion]

Edge Cases:
- [Edge case 1]: expected behavior: ...

sources.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
sources.excerpt: Sometimes I ask product managers to just go create a double sheet, have different tabs and what's the current behavior, what's the ideal behavior and why, and some notes. And sometimes they usually use it with evals, sometimes we use it for training. Because if you give the spreadsheet to o1 model, it can probably figure out how to teach itself a good behavior.
